steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The underlined claim is that Bianchini's calculations contain the earliest example of a decimal point. To support this, a finding must show an actual dot in Bianchini's work appearing in a numerical context that indicates a non-whole value — the defining feature of a decimal point.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'dots appear in both works but were commonly used to separate letters or whole numbers': This undermines the claim by suggesting the dots aren't decimal points at all. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Clavius gives examples using decimal numbers in Astrolabium': This is about Clavius, not Bianchini, and does nothing to establish Bianchini's priority. ✗
  • Choice C — 'in Bianchini's 1404 Tabulae primi mobilis B, a dot appears between two numbers suggesting a non-whole value': This directly shows Bianchini using a dot as a decimal point in an astronomical table — exactly the evidence the claim requires. ✓
  • Choice D — 'some of Bianchini's calculations are inaccurate due to using whole numbers instead of decimals': This shows Bianchini sometimes failed to use decimals, which weakens rather than supports the claim. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
